key_insight: A causal-adversarial framework can test whether prostate MRI grading models rely on clinical covariates that are helpful disease signals or harmful shortcuts.
clinical_relevance: Suppressing non-informative covariates improved generalization, while suppressing PSA and prostate volume reduced performance, suggesting this method could help build more robust prostate MRI grading models.
method: Model MRI appearance and ISUP grade as observations of latent tumor pathology.
Adversarially suppress the decodability of one clinical covariate at a time while preserving grade prediction.
Compare performance changes to classify variables as nuisance correlates, disease-related proxies, or irrelevant covariates.
evidence_signal: Evaluated on 2,903 prostate MRI exams with external validation on 576 patients; suppressing age, BMI, and alcohol use improved AUC, whereas suppressing PSA and prostate volume worsened AUC.
watch_item: Performance shifts indicate representation dependence, but they do not prove true causality and may vary across sites, scanners, and patient populations.
paper_title: Causal-Adversarial Probing of Clinical Covariates for Prostate MRI Grading
